title: Chapter 1: Data Analysis With Python Course - Quiz
description: Test your understanding of data analysis with pandas, data cleaning, and visualization

questions.question: What is the primary difference between a pandas Series and a DataFrame?

options.text: A Series can hold multiple data types while a DataFrame cannot
A Series is a one-dimensional labeled array, while a DataFrame is a two-dimensional labeled data structure with columns of potentially different types
A DataFrame is faster than a Series for all operations
A Series can only hold numeric data while a DataFrame can hold any type

questions.explanation: A pandas Series is a one-dimensional labeled array that can hold any data type, similar to a column in a spreadsheet. A DataFrame is a two-dimensional labeled data structure — essentially a table — where each column is a Series. DataFrames can have columns of different data types.

questions.question: Which pandas function is used to read a CSV file into a DataFrame?
questions.explanation: pd.read_csv() is the standard pandas function for reading CSV files into a DataFrame. It supports many parameters for handling different delimiters, encodings, missing values, column types, and more.

questions.question: How do you handle missing values in a pandas DataFrame by removing rows that contain any NaN?
questions.explanation: df.dropna() removes rows (by default) that contain any NaN values. You can also pass axis=1 to drop columns with missing values, or use the 'how' parameter ('any' or 'all') and the 'subset' parameter to control the behavior more precisely.

questions.question: What does the groupby() method do in pandas?

options.text: It sorts the DataFrame by the specified column
It splits the data into groups based on some criteria, applies a function to each group, and combines the results
It merges two DataFrames together based on a common column
It creates a copy of the DataFrame grouped into chunks of equal size

questions.explanation: The groupby() method implements the split-apply-combine pattern: it splits the DataFrame into groups based on column values, applies an aggregation function (like mean, sum, count) to each group independently, and then combines the results back into a DataFrame.

questions.question: Which method is used to combine two DataFrames based on a common column, similar to a SQL JOIN?
questions.explanation: pd.merge() combines DataFrames based on common columns or indices, similar to SQL JOIN operations. It supports inner, outer, left, and right joins through the 'how' parameter. pd.concat() is used for stacking DataFrames along an axis rather than joining on keys.

questions.question: What does a pivot table do in pandas?

options.text: It reshapes data by turning unique values from one column into separate columns, summarizing data with an aggregation function
It permanently reorders all columns alphabetically
It converts all data types to strings

questions.explanation: A pivot table reshapes data by using unique values from one or more columns as new column headers, while aggregating the values using a function like mean, sum, or count. It is created using df.pivot_table() and is useful for summarizing and cross-tabulating data.

questions.question: Which matplotlib function creates a basic line plot?
questions.explanation: plt.plot() creates a line plot by default when given x and y data. plt.bar() creates bar charts, plt.scatter() creates scatter plots, and plt.hist() creates histograms. Line plots are useful for displaying trends over time or continuous data.

questions.question: What is seaborn's relationship to matplotlib?

options.text: Seaborn is a complete replacement for matplotlib with no dependency on it
Seaborn is a high-level statistical visualization library built on top of matplotlib that provides a more attractive default style and convenient functions
Seaborn is a JavaScript library that converts matplotlib charts to interactive web visualizations
Seaborn is an older version of matplotlib that is no longer maintained

questions.explanation: Seaborn is built on top of matplotlib and provides a higher-level interface for creating attractive statistical graphics. It integrates well with pandas DataFrames, offers better default aesthetics, and includes specialized plot types like violin plots, heatmaps, and pair plots that are more complex to create in plain matplotlib.

questions.question: How do you fill missing values in a pandas DataFrame column with the column's mean?
questions.explanation: df['col'].fillna(value) replaces all NaN values in the column with the specified value. Using df['col'].mean() as the fill value is a common imputation strategy. Other strategies include filling with the median, mode, or a constant value.

questions.question: What does df.describe() return?

options.text: The first 5 rows of the DataFrame
The column names and data types of the DataFrame
Summary statistics including count, mean, standard deviation, min, quartiles, and max for numeric columns
A detailed description of the data source and how it was collected

questions.explanation: df.describe() generates descriptive statistics for numeric columns by default, including count, mean, std (standard deviation), min, 25th percentile, 50th percentile (median), 75th percentile, and max. You can include non-numeric columns by passing include='all'.
